People often know just enough to get themselves and others, including you, into trouble. This is especially true if they've wrestled with something for years or decades, or have researched themselves into a hissy fit, which they end up taking out on you. The point is, a practitioner takes quality information for granted. To everybody else, they can't tell if it's quality or not. Good information, this is odd, good information just adds to the noise, and the general population sees this stuff as conflicting evidence. There isn't a whole lot of conflicting evidence. There's just good information and crappy information, and people can't tell the difference. That being said, this section is a multiple fold purpose. One, helping address science questions and misinformation, two, helping navigate through a client's false and possibly unhealthy belief patterns. Like I said, as an experienced practitioner, anybody in this field or anybody in any field where they deal with people that can smell BS from a mile away. However, you also know you can't just say that it's BS because you simultaneously insult the client and also become one of them. Gross. Guiding the client toward clarity is superior to positioning yourself as an expert through facts, correcting people, things of that nature. They already know you're an expert. So reminding them that you're an expert is demeaning. Clients will speak and write to you and frequently reference things they believe. That's good. That means they trust you. However, it will be common for these beliefs to stem from misinformation. So sit on your hands and bite your tongue for now. And if you're being a silly goose, the superior information does zip Ola to help them. It is not your job to write the wrongs. It is your job to shrink the intention intervention gap. If the client wants to hear from you explicitly, they will ask if you want to provide input, get explicit permission. If there's one thing you learn from this, that is it always get permission. Even if the misinformation is brought up because a client wants to know your opinion, it does not mean you're given a license to plow the road. It is still your duty to verify explicitly the client wants your input. If they agree and they want to expand on insert BS here, then you can give a concise explanation in plain English of the phenomena they're speaking about. After that, make sure to get their feelings about the response you gave. So being a little more specific with the previous example, it's clear you're putting your health first and currently improving your body composition. You were speaking a bit about using a ketogenic diet twice per week to speed up fat loss. And I think it would be fruitful if we expanded on that more. And to which the client says, sounds good to me. And so as a practitioner, you respond with, by decreasing your carbohydrates twice per week, you decrease the number of calories you eat on average. By eating fewer calories than you were before, you ended up losing weight. Tell me what you think about this explanation. To which the client says, I think it makes sense. When you think about it, it appears diet methods fall along the same guidelines by accidentally directing people to eat less food. Do you see this trend or any other trend? And the client says, OMG, you are totally correct. All these fad diets just seem to trick people into eating less. This is crazy. That's essentially how a belief breaking conversation goes when it comes to things like science misinformation. Okay, the whole point is that you get their permission. See what they know about the topic and then get permission. Figure out exactly what the client is trying to accomplish, respond in regular English, follow the bikini rule, remember just enough to satisfy the requirements. So a couple of ground rules here were three ground rules. One, don't be a science Nazi. The client already assumes you're an expert being fancy with your words, just belittles them. And then second part of this is that there's always a better science sir out there. Keep that in mind. All right. Number two, take the client's inquiries and beliefs seriously. If you insult their beliefs, the methods, the articles or authors at your client references, then you are insulting the client. And by extension, you may also be insulting their family and or friends. Secondarily, false beliefs about health and in an unhealthy situation. It commonly serves as a proxy for some deeper issue. Keep an eye out. In other words, they may just be clinging on to something they think they can control because something else in their life is out of whack. Is this your problem to solve? No. Is it your job to keep an eye out for weird stuff like this? Absolutely. And lastly, if you are making, if you are being asked about something, make sure you and the client fully understand what is being asked of you and that you have their explicit permission to respond. If a person just wants to get your thoughts on something, which is probably my biggest pet peeve on planet fricking earth, that is not actionable or useful for anybody. It's just maddening. Get to the point specifically what they would want out of you in terms of an answer and how it is actionable. Make sure the client is explicit about what problem they're trying to solve. Clarify exactly how they think whatever this is applies to them. Most of the time it doesn't. And they're just genuinely curious about something, which is fine. It's just good to lay all that stuff out to get the most value out of the conversation for the both of you. So give a little roadmap here to guiding clients to misinformation. Step one, you hear misinformation that makes you so tilted you'd rather listen to Nickelback. Right. That is step one. Step two, ask the client what they know about the topic. Step three, ask what concerns or beliefs they have based on what they know. Step four, this is where you guide or you gauge the client's depth of knowledge and interest. And this lets you know what sort of language or how advanced of the language you should use when you respond to their question. If you figure out that the client has a highly technical background in the question that they're asking, then you can match the language. If they don't, then don't five ask if you can talk about it with them explicit permission to be involved in the conversation. Because if you just throw facts back at them, then you're correcting them and nobody likes that. Okay. And then if they care or if they agree to let you expand, then give a short contextually relevant explanation that uses plain English. Do not say the client is wrong. Do not correct the client. Do not insult the resource method or article. They are referencing the whole point is for you to provide clarity. And then after you provide them with clarity or try to the last step is see how they feel about the explanation you gave. So you provide your input and then how do they feel about the input? If you can make it through an exchange like that without taking a rusty spoon to scoop out your brain, then you are well on your way to expert status. It's very good. This sort of motivational style motivational interview style dialogue. This will be the cornerstone of your interactions when it comes to misinformation.
